name: Wine
summary: Still and sparkling wine, $10-$500/bottle range. Heavy marketing claim / reality gap.

evidence.claim: Wine ratings from major critics (Parker, Wine Spectator) correlate weakly with blind-tasting preferences across consumers; price-band placebo effect is well documented in behavioral-economics literature.
'Reserve' has no legal meaning in US wine labeling; it's purely marketing. In Italy (Riserva) and Spain (Reserva) it has legal aging requirements.

confabulationPatterns.reality: US: no legal meaning (E2). Only the Italian 'Riserva' and Spanish 'Reserva' have regulated aging requirements. US 'Reserve' is marketing.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is this from a jurisdiction where Reserve/Riserva has legal meaning, or US where it doesn't?

confabulationPatterns.reality: Critic ratings correlate weakly with blind-tasting preferences across individuals (E1). Score placement between 90-95 has minor real correlation; point-level differences in the 90s are often statistical noise.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: Is the score framing premium-differentiation or baseline quality?

confabulationPatterns.reality: Specific; French oak vs American oak does produce measurable flavor differences. But 'in' could mean 5% neutral oak contact vs 100% new-barrel fermentation — huge spectrum.
confabulationPatterns.checkPrompt: What percentage new oak? What duration?

counterfeitSignals.signal: High-end first-growth Bordeaux priced at auction well below market
counterfeitSignals.action: flag — counterfeit fine wine is a documented problem in the secondary market

compatibilityQuestions: Food pairing? Matters more than critic score.
Current drinking or cellar? Vintage and producer matter if cellaring.
Subjective palate preference (fruit-forward, earthy, tannic) matters more than price.
